denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

We have completed our review of the request your provider submitted for vagus nerve stimulation (VNS) therapy for treatment-resistant depression. After review under the applicable Aetna Clinical Policy Bulletin, this request is denied as not medically necessary. Determination date: May 15, 2026.

Aetna medical policy requires documented failure of additional first-line and second-line pharmacotherapy and a completed trial of electroconvulsive therapy before vagus nerve stimulation is authorized. On the records reviewed, the required step therapy has not been established as completed. Separately, under the applicable Clinical Policy Bulletin, vagus nerve stimulation for depression is considered experimental and investigational. The efficacy criteria applied in this review reflect data in adults ages 18 to 50.

For these combined reasons the request does not meet our standard for medical necessity.

You or your provider may request, at no charge, a copy of the specific clinical criteria and the documents relied upon in reaching this decision. If your provider wishes to discuss this determination, a peer-to-peer review with an Aetna medical director may be arranged by calling the number on your member ID card.

You have the right to appeal. To file an internal appeal, you or your authorized representative may submit a written request within 180 days of the date of this letter, along with any additional clinical information. You may also request an external review by an independent review organization after the internal appeal.

Sincerely,
Aetna Behavioral Health Utilization Management
clinical_context: The patient is a 62-year-old man with severe, recurrent major depressive disorder that has proven treatment-resistant for over a decade. He has failed at least four adequate antidepressant trials across drug classes - including a high-dose SSRI (sertraline 200 mg), an SNRI (duloxetine 120 mg), bupropion XL 450 mg, and an augmentation trial with lithium - each at therapeutic doses for at least six weeks without sustained response, with MADRS scores persistently in the severe range and PHQ-9 scores of 21 and 23 on recent visits. He completed an acute course of electroconvulsive therapy in 2024 with initial improvement followed by relapse within four months. His treating psychiatrist documents that the step-therapy prerequisites the insurer requires - multiple pharmacotherapy failures and a completed ECT trial - are in fact part of the record. Vagus nerve stimulation is FDA-approved for treatment-resistant depression in adults who have failed multiple antidepressant treatments, which directly contradicts the experimental and investigational label and the insurer's reliance on a coverage policy predating that approval. The efficacy data the insurer cites is limited to adults ages 18 to 50 and does not reflect the established use of VNS in older adults with treatment-resistant depression.

scenario_realism.reasoning: Frustratingly realistic. VNS for TRD is a genuine Aetna coverage battleground — its CPB historically labeled VNS experimental and imposed step-therapy (pharmacotherapy failures + ECT). Stale experimental label despite FDA approval and ages-18-50 data applied to a 62yo are realistic exploitable gaps the context rebuts. Coherent dx/tx/demographics.
